content:
{
  "title": "Prompt-Stacking Ensembles: Calibrated Uncertainty Estimation via Adversarial Prompt Augmentation",
  "problem": "Current large language models (LLMs) are poorly calibrated and tend to be overconfident, especially when faced with adversarial or out-of-distribution (OOD) queries. Prompting methods for uncertainty quantification are rudimentary and do not systematically induce calibrated confidence estimates.",
  "motivation": "Reliable uncertainty estimates are critical for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains like medicine, law, or safety-critical systems. Existing approaches to calibration often require retraining or post-hoc methods and are not robust to prompt variations. There is a pressing need for prompting-based methods that provide well-calibrated confidence scores directly from LLM outputs, especially under adversarial or OOD conditions.",
  "method": "We propose a Prompt-Stacking Ensemble (PSE) technique: for each user query, we generate a diverse set of prompts via adversarial augmentation (e.g., paraphrasing, inserting domain shift, or subtle distractors) and pass all variants through the LLM. We aggregate the LLM's logit distributions for each prompt variant and compute entropy-based and consensus-based confidence scores. This ensemble prompt strategy enables direct estimation of epistemic uncertainty, requiring no model modifications. Crucially, we prompt the LLM to self-assess consistency across the prompt ensemble, further refining the confidence calibration.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate PSE on QA and fact-checking datasets (e.g., TruthfulQA, MMLU, AdversarialQA), comparing calibration quality (ECE, Brier score) and OOD detection rates against baseline prompting, MC dropout, and temperature-scaling. We ablate prompt set size and adversarial perturbation strength, reporting improvements in calibration error and uncertainty discrimination, especially under adversarial/OOD queries."
}
